# Go test coverage snapshot — 2026-04-16

**Generated by:** overnight maintenance loop (bod 5 — coverage report).
**Command:** `go test -short -cover -count=1 ./...`
**Purpose:** Surface the packages carrying the most untested risk so
that targeted test-writing PRs can be prioritised. This is a report
only — no fixes are in scope for the run that produced it.

Re-run the command above before acting on any line here; coverage
numbers drift as code and tests land in parallel.

### Packages with **zero** test files

| Package | Files | Purpose |
|---------|-------|---------|
| `internal/keeper` | `types.go` | Top-level Keeper types (facade over sub-packages). |
| `internal/provider` | `container.go`, `state.go`, `storage.go` | Provider-interface declarations. |
| `internal/services/onboarding` | `onboarding.go` | First-run onboarding flow. |
| `internal/terminal` | `handler.go` | Terminal session handler. |

`cmd/*` and `tools/*` binaries are expected to run as untested entry
points — covered by integration / e2e instead.

## Recommended focus order

The two "🔴 critical" packages stand out for the mismatch between
**production criticality** and **coverage floor**.

1. **`internal/ws` (8.3%)** — WebSocket hub is the fan-out point for
   every realtime UI event (missions, assignments, escalations).
   Regressions here silently break the dashboard without any server
   error. Worth a focused PR: hub lifecycle, channel auth, broadcast
   partitioning, concurrent subscribe/unsubscribe.

2. **`internal/provider/docker` (9.6%)** — The container provider sits
   directly behind every crew/agent run. `docker.go` is ~large and
   handles network setup (`ensureNetwork`, `Internal: false` invariant
   documented in MEMORY.md), image pulls, exec, pause/unpause (backup
   path). Two test files exist but clearly leave most of the surface
   untouched. Integration-flavored tests (using the existing
   `resilience_integration_test.go` as a template) are the right shape.

3. **`internal/terminal/handler.go`** — No tests at all. If the UI
   terminal feature is still in active use, even a single happy-path
   exec test would raise the floor.

4. **`internal/services/onboarding.go`** — Zero coverage despite being
   the first thing a new install touches. A failure here greets every
   fresh user.

5. **`internal/api` (18.7%)** — Coverage percentage is misleading
   because the package is 113 `.go` files / 22 `*_test.go`; the absolute
   untested line count is the largest in the tree. Likely candidates:
   bulk admin handlers added after the initial api test suite
   (`backup.go`, `captain_*.go`, `issue_handler_*.go`,
   `integrations.go`, `project_handler.go`, `mission_handler.go`).

## Out of scope for this run

- **No test writing.** The overnight brief limits run 5 to reporting.
- **No refactors across packages.** Lifting coverage on any of the
  above is its own focused PR.
- **No frontend coverage.** This report is Go-only; `pnpm test` /
  Vitest coverage belongs to a separate audit.
